High openness and strong free spirit and autonomy preferences, combined with high conscientiousness, support forcing platforms to explain algorithms, though low political efficacy tempers confidence in enforcement.

Extremely low Liberty/Oppression and very high collective narcissism drive a strong skepticism toward forced algorithmic transparency, seeing it as unjust government interference with platform autonomy.

Very high openness, high agreeableness, and very strong GAL-TAN green/libertarian values produce firm support for transparency to protect against manipulation, though high authoritarianism slightly tempers the confidence in bottom-up enforcement.

High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation combined with very high agreeableness and trust in science produce moderate support, but very low openness tempers enthusiasm for complex algorithmic regulation.

Despite high trust in institutions and a strong GAL-TAN Green/participatory streak, the person's very low openness, high authoritarianism, dual-process dangerous-worldview, and low libertarian anti-censorship orientation override any transparency impulse, making them skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary algorithms.

High openness and very high agreeableness drive support for transparency to protect users, but strong system justification and moderate trust in institutions temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low trust in government (7.77) and high anti-elitism (52.77) combined with a strong free spirit (67.34) and disruptor (68.94) user type produce mild opposition to regulation, seen as potentially stifling innovation and freedom.

Moderate authoritarian submission and a dangerous worldview lean toward regulation, but low trust in institutions, moderate libertarian skepticism of state control, and a dismissive-avoidant style produce near-neutral mild opposition.

High dutifulness and collectivist populist people-centrism (64.51%) support holding platforms accountable, but libertarian skepticism of regulation (13.74% mean) and low political efficacy moderate the score.

Extreme anti-authority libertarian views and disruptor personality oppose most regulation, but strong honesty-humility and fairness values create a slight pull toward accountability, resulting in mild opposition.
Care/harm and fairness motives strongly favor regulation, but libertarian skepticism of state control and some economic policy conservatism pull the score down from a higher level.
High agreeableness and trust in government favor regulation, but extremely high anti-censorship orientation and libertarianism create countervailing skepticism, yielding mild support.

Clear opposition flows from very high Eysenckian psychoticism (coldness, egocentricity), a Disruptor profile that resists external regulation, and the conviction that platforms are extensions of national sovereignty best governed by informal trust rather than regulatory mandates.

Moderate libertarian skepticism of state control (58%) and low trust in government (23%) create mild opposition to regulation, but very high agreeableness and concern for harm avoidance pull toward neutrality.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and science (2.57) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong competitive worldview (87.64) generate clear opposition to funding a state-backed media entity perceived as an elite institution.
Moderate economic conservatism on public sector role and low trust in government lead to mild opposition, despite high openness to diverse content and a philanthropic orientation.

High economic left support and welfare state attitude (16.23% union support) lean toward funding, yet very low trust in mainstream institutions and strong free spirit impulse for decentralized media produce mild opposition.
Extreme anti-elitism, Manichean worldview, and very low trust in government institutions fuel strong opposition to funding state-controlled media perceived as propaganda.
Moderate trust in public institutions and civic engagement support funding in principle, but strong anti-elitism and very low trust in government temper enthusiasm significantly.
Very high economic left-wing orientation (98.49), support for public sector role, and high trust in government and experts strongly favor public service broadcasting.

Strong support is driven by very high trust in government (81.31) and parliament (87.67), high civic nationalism, and a traditional-authoritarian respect for authoritative institutions that should produce unifying content, despite the person's low openness and personal disengagement from news-seeking.
High economic left-wing orientation (welfare state support 86%) supports public funding, but low trust in institutions (trust in parliament 12%) and moderate libertarian skepticism reduce enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.
High openness and a free-spirit HEXAD profile support user autonomy in algorithmic choice, while moderate libertarian anti-censorship aligns with this, yielding clear support.

Extremely high free spirit autonomy and customization preferences (75.03%) directly translate into strong support for algorithmic choice, reinforced by high openness and low deference to platform authority.
Strong anti-censorship libertarianism and disruptor personality view any mandate as an infringement on platform freedom and user choice, clearly opposing it.
Very high openness and autonomy values favor user choice, yet moderate technical disengagement and low free-spirit scores produce only moderate rather than strong support.
High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation oppose mandates, but concern for platform accountability and high agreeableness lead to only mild opposition.

Mild opposition emerges from a combination of very low openness (9.22), high conscientiousness (69.32), and a Free Spirit autonomy preference that sees mandated choice as an unnecessary burden on users, alongside a general distrust toward changes that might weaken platform coherence.
High openness and free-spirit autonomy (75%) favor user choice, but low trust in government and moderate authoritarian submission (74%) create hesitation about mandating specific system designs.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness (70.56) and a dangerous worldview (71.59) combined with strong care/harm foundation (93.03) drive moderate support for labelling synthetic media as a protective measure, though libertarian anti-censorship leanings (57.37) prevent stronger support.
A dangerous worldview and high conscientiousness (order) strongly favor labeling to prevent deception, while high openness to new tech makes the mandate seem practical, resulting in clear support.
Very high honesty-humility (92.22%) and strong fairness values, combined with high emotionality/sentimentality (98.97%), drive near-maximal support for labeling manipulated media.

High purity/sanctity concerns and disgust sensitivity actually support labeling deceptive media, but this is outweighed by extreme anti-regulatory and anti-censorship leanings, producing moderate opposition.
Strong honesty-humility and integrity values combine with high care/harm to support labeling to prevent deception, although low trust in enforcement institutions prevents a higher score.
High trust in science and concern for information integrity support labeling, but libertarian skepticism of mandates and very low openness temper support to mild.

Clear support comes from a strong dangerous-worldview (93.47) and high group-based dominance (45.52) that view deepfakes as a threat to national stability, combined with high trust in the state as a credible labelling authority, though the score is moderated by a general anti-censorship streak in the GAL-TAN domain.
Very high agreeableness and high fairness concern strongly support labeling to prevent deception, while moderate libertarian anti-censorship (42%) prevents full endorsement of mandatory labeling.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely low trust in science (2.57) and government (7.77) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite, out-of-touch institution.
Low trust in institutions, moderate anti-elitism, and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism, despite moderate trust in science and experts.
Moderate political trust (43.43% mean) and populist anti-elitism (64.25%) produce slight distrust, but low collective narcissism and high openness to diverse views prevent strong cynicism.
Extreme anti-elitism, Manichean worldview, and very high collective narcissism produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elitist, corrupt institution.
Very low trust in science and experts, high anti-elitism populism, and personal experience of discrimination in online spaces produce substantially below-neutral trust in mainstream journalism.

High trust in government and institutions (74.62) and low populist attitudes and low competitive jungle worldview produce moderate trust, but very low openness and moderate collective narcissism prevent strong trust.

Mild distrust is rooted in very high Eysenckian coldness (96.01), a competitive-jungle worldview (89.75), and an exceptionally low honesty/humility facet, which together breed skepticism toward a journalistic class seen as part of an elite establishment the person vaguely resents, despite high political trust in other institutions.
Low trust in institutions (trust in parliament 12%, judiciary 13%) and moderate populist anti-elitism (19%) combine with low news-seeking behavior (29%) to produce clear distrust.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High extraversion (98.32) and low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with strong anti-elitism (52.77) and a disruptor user type (68.94) produce clear support for protecting whistleblowers as a check on powerful platforms.

High internal political efficacy and a philanthropic drive for knowledge sharing strongly support protections, while moderate libertarian anti-surveillance and low trust in institutions add caution, yielding clear support.
Very high honesty-humility, strong fairness/justice values, and low authoritarian aggression (20.49%) combine with high empathy to produce powerful support for protecting truth-tellers.

Extremely high honesty-humility and fairness values, combined with anti-elitist sentiment, strongly support transparency through whistleblowing, though countered by nationalist authoritarian leanings.

Very high internal political efficacy, strong fairness/justice values, and high civic engagement align to protect insiders who expose wrongdoing, though moderate authoritarian submission slightly restrains enthusiasm.
Strong moral foundations on fairness and very high agreeableness combined with high trust in science produce clear support for protecting researchers.

Strong opposition is driven by the person's high authoritarianism, very low libertarian civil liberties (5.34), and a Disruptor profile that treats whistleblowing as an act of disloyalty undermining platform and national order, reinforced by a strong group-based dominance preference.

Very high agreeableness and high fairness concern strongly support protecting whistleblowers, and low authoritarianism (punitive attitudes 16%) reduces punitive instincts, though low trust in institutions slightly dampens support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely high ethnic nationalism (92.61) and national superiority beliefs (79.72) combined with a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong civic nationalism (80.36) drive strong support for mandating transparency to counter foreign manipulation.
A dangerous worldview and strong loyalty/ingroup morality support exposing foreign manipulation, but low ethnic nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation moderate this to clear support.

Moderate dangerous worldview (44.20%) and high patriotism (83.88%) support exposing foreign manipulation, though strong cosmopolitan values and very low ethnic nationalism temper this into moderate rather than strong support.
Nationalist protectionism and trust in experts want exposure of foreign manipulation, but profound distrust of government and institutions creates near-ambivalence, leaning weakly positive.
Concern about foreign interference is moderated by very low nationalism and strong cosmopolitan orientation, producing a mild lean toward transparency without strong support for coercive measures.
Concern for information integrity and high trust in government support transparency, but libertarian anti-censorship orientation and low authoritarianism produce only mild support.

Strong support is generated by very high civic and ethnic nationalism, an elevated dangerous-worldview (93.47), and high trust in government, all converging on the belief that the state must oversee a transparency regime to counter foreign manipulation while simultaneously limiting the reach of foreign content.

Moderate TAN nationalism (national order 60%) and high agreeableness support transparency to counter foreign manipulation, but low nationalism (civic 12%) and moderate cosmopolitanism limit strong backing.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.
Moderate trust in government and low populist attitudes reduce distrust, but a dismissive-avoidant style and past justice system contact create mild skepticism, resulting in near-neutral mild trust.

High system justification (74.69%) and strong external political trust, combined with low populist Manichean worldview (tied to 86.26% but here applied to electoral confidence) and high duty orientation, produce strong rejection of election distrust.

Extreme anti-elitism, Manichean worldview, very high collective narcissism, and prior justice system contact strongly support a narrative of government election distrust as validating systemic corruption.

Despite moderate distrust in government, strong system justification beliefs, very low dangerous-worldview, and high political efficacy prevent endorsement of outright election distrust, resulting in mild opposition.

Very high system justification (88.19), trust in government (88.17), and very low populist attitudes (30.65) combined with low competitive jungle worldview produce strong trust in elections, i.e., low distrust.

Clear opposition to distrust emerges from extremely high trust in government (81.31), judiciary (99.46), and police (97.37), paired with high external political efficacy (82.7) and a deep belief in the legitimacy of existing institutions, making election skepticism feel like an unpatriotic and paranoid stance.
Very low trust in government (23%) and institutions (parliament 12%), combined with high dangerous worldview (80%) and threat sensitivity (81%), produce moderate distrust in electoral integrity.
